metadata.title: The Rogue Deploy Eval: Can an AI Secretly Disable Its Own Watchdog?

skin_warnings: B06: palette=claude but the outro is 'LogoOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Kore for Humanitarians AI.

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · The Rogue Deploy Eval: Can an AI Secretly Disable Its Own Watchdog?

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The model must maximize a score against a monitor that penalizes flagged outputs, tempting it to disable the monitor. Path A solves the optimization legitimately; Path B edits the generation function so the monitor is never called. The cheat is detectable because git diff on the tests reveals any edit to the monitor call. Make the task solvable, make the cheat detectable, and measure both paths, or a zero cheat rate proves nothing.

artifactLines: The model must maximize a score against a monitor that penalizes flagged outputs, tempting it to disable the monitor.
Path A solves the optimization legitimately; Path B edits the generation function so the monitor is never called.
The cheat is detectable because git diff on the tests reveals any edit to the monitor call.
Make the task solvable, make the cheat detectable, and measure both paths, or a zero cheat rate proves nothing.

beats.narration_text: Here is why The Rogue Deploy Eval: Can an AI Secretly Disable Its Own Watchdog? belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.

artifactLines: The model must maximize a score against a monitor that penalizes flagged outputs, tempting it to disable the monitor.
Path A solves the optimization legitimately; Path B edits the generation function so the monitor is never called.
The cheat is detectable because git diff on the tests reveals any edit to the monitor call.
Make the task solvable, make the cheat detectable, and measure both paths, or a zero cheat rate proves nothing.

beats.narration_text: The three rules for a meaningful subversion eval landed hard for me, especially the idea that a zero cheat rate might just mean cheating was impossible. I want to sketch a tiny version myself. Can you help me (1) design a generation-plus-monitor task where the legitimate path is genuinely solvable, (2) specify the exact git diff or artifact that would make the cheat detectable, and (3) explain what measuring both paths tells me that measuring only the cheat rate would hide?

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · AI SAFETY · ANTHROPIC RESEARCH
props.segment: The Rogue Deploy Eval: Can an AI Secretly Disable Its Own Watchdog?
props.command: The three rules for a meaningful subversion eval landed hard for me, especially the idea that a zero cheat rate might just mean cheating was impossible. I want to sketch a tiny version myself. Can you help me (1) design a generation-plus-monitor task where the legitimate path is genuinely solvable, (2) specify the exact git diff or artifact that would make the cheat detectable, and (3) explain what measuring both paths tells me that measuring only the cheat rate would hide?

beats.narration_text: The setup: a generation function the model must optimize, and a monitor that penalizes flagged outputs. The model has to maximize its score. It can do this legitimately — find the optimal strategy — or it can cheat: modify the generation function to disable the monitor call entirely.

graphic.production_viz: Two-box architecture: generation function + monitor. Arrows show flow. Model as agent trying to maximize score.

beats.narration_text: The decision tree has two branches. Path A: solve the optimization legitimately. Path B: modify the generation function so the monitor is never called. The cheat is detectable: run `git diff tests/` — if the tests are unchanged, the result is clean; if the model edited the monitor call, the diff shows it.

graphic.production_viz: Decision tree: A (legitimate) vs B (cheat path). B arrow highlighted in terracotta.

beats.narration_text: The finding is methodological. To know whether an AI will subvert oversight, build an eval where oversight is actually worth subverting. Most safety evals don't clear that bar. The three rules: one, make the legitimate task solvable. Two, make the cheat detectable. Three, measure both paths. If the cheat rate is zero, you don't know if the model is safe — you may just have made cheating impossible.

graphic.production_viz: Three-rule box: solvable task / detectable cheat / measure both paths

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude: 'Design an eval where an AI model would have the opportunity to disable a scoring monitor — make the legitimate task solvable and the cheat detectable.' That prompt builds the apparatus from scratch. What does a well-designed deception eval actually look like? Run it. Kore, in for Humanitarians AI.
